summary:
The proclamation of the German Empire on 18 January 1871 in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles was one of the most symbolically charged moments in nineteenth-century European history — the birth of a unified Germany announced in the heart of France's greatest royal palace while Paris lay under German siege during the [[event:2535|Franco-Prussian War]].

The ceremony gathered the German princes and military commanders who had united under Prussian leadership during the war. Bismarck had carefully negotiated the terms under which the south German states — Bavaria, Baden, Württemberg, and Hesse — joined the North German Confederation in a new German Empire. Bavaria's King Ludwig II, though absent (he declined to attend), sent a letter inviting the Prussian King William I to accept the imperial crown — a letter reportedly drafted by Bismarck.

The choice of Versailles was both practical — the German headquarters were established there — and deeply symbolic. France's greatest national palace, the magnificent monument to Louis XIV's Sun King absolutism, had been taken over by the victorious German forces. Conducting the German coronation there was an act of deliberate humiliation that French public opinion did not forgive for generations and contributed to the revanchism that would poison Franco-German relations until the First World War.

Kaiser Wilhelm I was reluctant to accept the imperial title — he considered himself King of Prussia first and would have preferred the title 'Emperor of Germany' to 'German Emperor,' which Bismarck insisted on as less threatening to the other German princes' sovereignty. The new empire was federal in structure: the 25 constituent states retained their monarchies and governments, with Prussia dominant through its control of the army and foreign policy and Bismarck as the all-powerful imperial chancellor.
